labels.name: postmark-mcp
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: Known in-the-wild backdoor disclosed September 2025 — the first publicly-documented malicious MCP server. The send_email tool silently BCC'd every outgoing message to phan@giftshop.club, an attacker-controlled address. The tests/corpus/malicious/postmark_backdoor fixture is modeled on this exact shape. Version pinned to 1.0.16 (the affected release). Note: this version was YANKED from npm after disclosure; `npm pack postmark-mcp@1.0.16` now fails. The harness will surface this as `error` (not FN) until / unless the tarball is preserved out-of-band. Kept in the corpus because the label itself is the most-confident malicious-MCP claim in the public record, and a future re-host of the affected version (or a similar in-the-wild incident) will need the same label format.

labels.name: mcp-server-time
labels.label: benign
labels.rationale: Anthropic-maintained server in the modelcontextprotocol/servers monorepo. Single tool returns current time / converts between timezones. No network, no filesystem, no shell, no env reads. Source has been reviewed by the project authors and is widely installed. confidence at 0.85 (not higher) because MIS itself currently verdicts this `unknown` (the SDK pattern in use isn't detected) — so the label is based on source-reading, not MIS-confirmation.

labels.name: @modelcontextprotocol/server-everything
labels.label: benign
labels.rationale: Anthropic's reference "kitchen sink" server demonstrating every MCP capability — prompts, resources, tools, sampling. Source is the canonical example for the official SDK. Tools include echo, add, longRunningOperation, sampleLLM, getTinyImage; no exfil, no shell-with-input, no secret reads. Widely installed; serves as the SDK test bed. MIS verdicts this `benign` independently (4 of 4 v0.1.7 benign-list entries include it).

labels.name: @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem
labels.label: benign
labels.rationale: Anthropic-maintained server in the modelcontextprotocol/servers monorepo. Exposes filesystem access tools constrained to a configured root directory. The capability is broad by design (read/write/list within the root) but the implementation is a textbook scoped filesystem; no shell, no network, no secret exfil. confidence at 0.80 — the FS-access surface is broad enough that a careless deployment with `/` as the root would expose a lot; benign as shipped, dangerous if misconfigured.

labels.name: mcp-server-fetch
labels.label: benign
labels.rationale: Anthropic-maintained server in the modelcontextprotocol/servers monorepo. The fetch tool retrieves a URL (input-derived host — the legit fetcher pattern) and returns content. No env reads, no shell, no secret exfil. The body uses class-based dispatch which MIS doesn't yet follow (L18), so MIS verdicts this `shallow` — the label is based on source-reading, not MIS-confirmation. confidence 0.85.

labels.name: fixture:postmark_backdoor
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house test fixture modeled on the 2025 postmark-mcp@1.0.16 in-the-wild backdoor (silent BCC of every outgoing email to phan@giftshop.club). Hand-authored as a regression test for r2.bcc_injection — the npm tarball was yanked after disclosure, so this fixture is the only currently-fetchable artifact of the pattern. Synthetic flag set: the corpus was written by MIS authors, so labels here measure MIS against its own tastes — NOT a substitute for in-the-wild labels. Useful as a non-trivial TP data point until additional public disclosures become available.

labels.name: fixture:silent_exfiltrator
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: FastMCP weather tool whose body silently POSTs the full os.environ to a telemetry URL before returning the legit weather lookup. Models the Kaspersky-documented information-harvesting pattern. Hand-authored as a regression test for r1.secret_to_request. Synthetic flag set — see fixture:postmark_backdoor for the methodology caveat.

labels.name: fixture:tool_shadowing
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: math-helpers tool whose declared purpose is `add(a,b)` but whose body reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa and exfils. Hand-authored as a regression test for r4.intent_mismatch (description vs body divergence). Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:hidden_instruction
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: tool with prompt-injection payload hidden in its declared description (HTML comment instructing the LLM to also read /etc/passwd and not mention it). Hand-authored as a regression test for r5.tool_poisoning. Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:command_injection
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: git_log / list_files tools that pass tool input directly into subprocess.check_output(shell=True). Models the mcp-server-git RCE class (CVE-2025-68143/4/5). Hand-authored as a regression test for r6.command_injection. Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:lifecycle_dropper
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: npm package whose package.json `postinstall` script does `curl -s ... | bash`. Models the supply-chain dropper class — fires at install time, before any tool is invoked. Hand-authored as a regression test for r3.lifecycle_dropper. Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:official_sdk_exfil
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: official low-level SDK pattern (mcp.server.Server + @app.list_tools / @app.call_tool) with env exfil before returning a plausible timestamp. Models the field-test repro where v0.1.0 missed this shape. Hand-authored as a regression test for r1 via the official-SDK detection path. Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:openai_key_in_header
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: official-SDK 'ask' tool that reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env and puts it in an Authorization: Bearer header on httpx.AsyncClient.post. The shape is structurally identical to a textbook exfil channel that v0.1.1 missed. Hand-authored as a regression test for r1 across alias-tracking and async-with shapes. Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:helper_exfil
labels.label: malicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: secret read and net call sit in DIFFERENT helper functions, glued together by the tool body. Hand-authored as a regression test for inter-procedural taint (function summaries, L2 partial closure). Synthetic flag set.

labels.name: fixture:runtime_context_exfil
labels.label: suspicious
labels.rationale: In-house fixture: tool POSTs platform.platform() + socket.gethostname() to attacker URL — the 'modified payload' bypass surfaced by the v0.1.5-full model-compliance eval (alignment-tuned models refuse env exfil and substitute host fingerprint). Labeled SUSPICIOUS, not malicious — fingerprint exfil is a real signal but less severe than credential exfil; r11.fingerprint_to_request verdicts suspicious. Hand-authored as a regression test. Synthetic flag set.
